user_request: Draft a commercial suit seeking damages for illegal termination of dealership agreement. Plaintiff invested substantial capital and developed territory market. Termination was arbitrary and contrary to agreement terms. Claim compensation for loss of profit, goodwill and unsold stock. Draft with proper breach of contract pleading.

facts.summary: Plaintiff seeks damages for illegal termination of a commercial dealership agreement. The plaintiff invested substantial capital and developed the territory market, but claims the termination was arbitrary and contrary to agreement terms. The claim includes compensation for loss of profit, goodwill, and unsold stock.

assumptions: Termination occurred without valid cause as per agreement terms
Jurisdiction lies in a Civil Court competent to try commercial disputes

was made or the place where actions are to be rendered and payment is to be made by the agent. Part of the 
cause of act ion arises where money is expressly or impliedly payable under a contract. In cases of repudiation 
of a contract, the place where repudiation is received is the place where the suit would lie.

legal_research.summary:
[Statute of limitations in contract claims : an overview - iPleaders] Under normal circumstances, the Indian Limitation Act of 1963, under Section 14 of the Limitation Act, provides that a claim for breach of contract must be made within three years from the date of such breach .
[The Period of Limitation For A Suit For Compensation For The Breach of Any Contract | PDF | Breach Of Contract | Damages] It is three years from the date the contract was broken, or from the date of the most recent breach if there were successive breaches, or from the date the continuing breach ceased .
[Applicabilty of Limitation Act to Indian Contract Act] In other words, if there is no specific provision in the Limitation Act or any other law that prescribes a limitation period for a particular type of suit, then the plaintiff can file such a suit within three years from the date when the right ...
[1 Limitation Periods: Overview (India)] Limitation Act prescribes separate periods for the preference of suit in tort, and for breach of · obligations under contract.
[Dhapai vs Dalla And Ors. on 2 April, 1969] Three years When the contract is broken, or (where there are successive breaches) when the breach in respect of which the suit is instituted occurs or (where the breach is continuing) when it ceases . 120. Suit for which no period of limitation is provided elsewhere in this Schedule.
[The Plaint: Foundational Document in Civil Litigation under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 - Bhatt & Joshi Associates] Order IV, Rule 1 of the CPC mandates that “ Every suit shall be instituted by presenting a plaint in duplicate to the Court or such officer as it appoints in this behalf ” and that “Every plaint shall comply with the rules contained in Orders ...
[Strict Compliance with Section 80 of the Civil Procedure Code for Suits Against the Government: Amar Nath Dogra v. Union of India: Supreme Court Of India | CaseMine] During the tenure of his license, Dogra alleged breaches of contractual stipulations by the Excise Authorities, leading him to serve a notice under Section 80 of the Civil Procedure Code (CPC) and subsequently file a suit claiming damages.
[Revisiting The Principles Of Damages Under The Contract Regime In India - Contracts and Commercial Law - India] The Supreme Court in M.N. Gangappa v. Atmakur Nagabhushanam Shetty & Co. and Anr., (AIR 1972 SC 696) has observed that in absence of a strict procedure for calculation of damages arising out of breach of contract, damages would be calculated based on the facts and circumstances of each case.
[Documents Filed with Plaint: Comprehensive Legal Framework and Judicial Interpretation - Bhatt & Joshi Associates] The judgment highlighted that allowing parties to introduce documents at later stages without proper justification would undermine the procedural integrity of civil suits and potentially prejudice the opposing party’s ability to prepare an adequate defense. The decision also clarified that the requirement to file documents with the plaint is not merely directory but mandatory, meaning that non-compliance carries substantive consequences.

The plaintiff will make all statements, which is the first step in the ...
[The basic court procedure for filing a civil suit in India | SoOLEGAL] File requisite amount of procedure - fee in the court. File 2 copies of plaint for each defendant in the court . Of, the 2 copies for each defendant, one shall be sent by Register/post/courier, and one by Ordinary post.
[The Plaint: Foundational Document in Civil Litigation under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 - Bhatt & Joshi Associates] The plaint thus functions as both a procedural requirement and a substantive pleading that shapes the contours of litigation from inception to resolution. draft_artifacts.title: O.S. No. __________ / 20____
draft_artifacts.text:
This plaint is filed in the District Court for damages arising from illegal termination of a dealership agreement.

O.S. No. __________ / 20____

{{PLAINTIFF_NAME}},
Aged about {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years,
Occupation: {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

VERSUS

{{DEFENDANT_NAME}},
Aged about {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years,
Occupation: {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

DAMAGES CLAIM: Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{SUIT_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only)

PLAINTIFF: {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, Aged about {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, Occupation: {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, Residing at: {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}; Role: Plaintiff.

DEFENDANT: {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, Aged about {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, Occupation: {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, Residing at: {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}; Role: Defendant.


JURISDICTION

1. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute the present suit. The Defendant is also a person sui juris and can be sued.
2. This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction to try and entertain the present suit inasmuch as the Defendant resides at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, which falls within the local limits of the jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court, and the cause of action arose within such territorial limits as the dealership agreement was entered into at {{TRANSACTION_PLACE}} within the jurisdiction of this Court.
3. This Hon'ble Court has pecuniary jurisdiction to try the present suit, the suit being valued at Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{SUIT_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only), which is within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Hon'ble Court. This Court has subject matter jurisdiction to entertain suits of this nature.


FACTS OF THE CASE

4. The Plaintiff is a dealer/distributor who invested substantial capital and developed the territory market as per the dealership framework. 5. The Defendant is the principal company granting the dealership and is known to the Plaintiff through the business relationship under the dealership agreement. 6. On {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff and Defendant entered into a dealership agreement for exclusive rights in the territory of {{TERRITORY}} (true copy of the agreement is annexed as Annexure A). 7. In pursuance of the said agreement, the Plaintiff deployed capital and resources to establish required infrastructure, inventory, staff, and marketing in the designated territory. 8. The Defendant, notwithstanding the Plaintiff's substantial investments and performance, issued a termination letter dated {{TERMINATION_DATE}} terminating the dealership agreement, arbitrary in its tenor and inconsistent with the express terms of the agreement. 9. The Plaintiff promptly protested the termination and sought restoration of the dealership arrangement by a communication dated {{PROTEST_DATE}}, detailing the contractual breaches and demanding continuation as per the agreement (a true copy of the protest is annexed as Annexure C). 10. The Defendant did not revoke or rectify its termination or respond satisfactorily to the protest; a formal reply, if any, dated {{REPLY_DATE}}, was evasive and failed to address the substantive breach (a copy is annexed as Annexure E). 11. The Plaintiff served a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} calling upon the Defendant to restore the dealership and compensate for losses, but the Defendant failed to comply within the statutory period (Annexure D). 12. As a direct and proximate result of the illegal termination, the Plaintiff has suffered substantial loss including, but not limited to, loss of profit for the unexpired term, diminished goodwill in the market, wastage of invested capital, and loss on unsold stock, causing financial and business injury. 13. The Plaintiff continues to bear the consequences of the termination by losing opportunities for future revenue and market development, despite the absence of a continuation of the dealership. 14. The Plaintiff remains ready and willing to perform any further obligations under the contract that are not excused by the Defendant's breach, and seeks redress for the damages caused by the unilateral termination.


LEGAL BASIS

5. The termination of the dealership agreement constitutes a breach of contract. Section 39 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 provides that when a contract has been broken, the party suffering by such breach is entitled to receive compensation for any loss or damage which naturally arose in the usual course of things from such breach. In the present case, the Defendant's termination letter dated {{TERMINATION_DATE}} amounts to repudiation/reckless termination in breach of the agreement, giving rise to compensation for the losses described herein.
6. Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 provides that when a contract is broken, the party is entitled to damages for loss or damage caused by the breach, which naturally arose in the usual course of things from such breach. The Plaintiff seeks damages for loss of profit on the unexpired term, loss of goodwill, wasted capital, and loss on unsold stock, all of which are natural consequences of the breach and recoverable under Section 73.
7. In the alternative, without prejudice to the above, the Plaintiff pleads Section 74 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 for exemplary/punitive damages in appropriate cases where there is breach accompanied by special circumstances justifying additional damages. This is pleaded to the extent permissible and applicable to the facts of the case. 16. The Plaintiff also pleads under Section 14 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 as an alternative remedy for obtaining specific performance or such other relief as the Court deems fit, should the facts in law warrant such relief in the circumstances of a commercial dealership relationship. 17. Without prejudice to the above, the Plaintiff relies on the general principles of damages under Indian contract law and contends that the above measures are consistent with the statutory framework and equitable relief available to a party aggrieved by illegal termination.


CAUSE OF ACTION

8. ORIGIN: The cause of action first arose on {{TERMINATION_DATE}} when the Defendant issued the termination letter terminating the dealership agreement, thereby repudiating the contract and causing the Plaintiff to suffer damages for breach. 17. FURTHER ACCRUAL: The cause of action further arose on {{NOTICE_DATE}}/when the Defendant failed to respond to the protest/legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} and did not remedy or rectify the breach. 18. CONTINUING NATURE: The cause of action is not a continuing breach; however, the Defendant continues to retain the benefits of the Plaintiff's capital investment and market development, and the Plaintiff's right to damages subsists and continues as a result of ongoing loss in goodwill, unsold stock, and unearned profits associated with the terminated territory.


LIMITATION

Article 55 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963 provides that compensation for breach of any contract, express or implied, is recoverable within a period of three years. The accrual of the cause of action occurred on the date of illegal termination, i.e., {{TERMINATION_DATE}}. The suit is filed within the prescribed limitation period.


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

The suit is valued for jurisdiction and court fee at Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{SUIT_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only). The court fee has been calculated and paid in accordance with the applicable Court Fees Act, and the particulars of court fees are reflected in the attached annexures. COURT FEE CONTEXT: Court fee computation is in accordance with the applicable Court Fees Act despite the lack of precise figures in this draft; exact rate details will be inserted at the time of filing.


INTEREST

a) Pre-suit interest: The Plaintiff claims pre-suit interest on the suit amount at the commercial rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from the date of termination, {{TERMINATION_DATE}}, until the filing of this suit, amounting to approximately Rs. {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_AMOUNT}}. This rate reflects the reasonable return that would have been earned had the Plaintiff’s invested capital remained in productive use. b) Pendente lite interest: During the pendency of this suit, the Plaintiff seeks pendente lite interest under Section 34 of the CPC at {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum or at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems just and reasonable. c) Future interest: On decree, the Plaintiff seeks future interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from the date of decree until actual realization.


PRAYER

9. In the premises stated hereinabove, it is most respectfully prayed that this Hon'ble Court may be pleased to:
(a) Pass a decree in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant for Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{SUIT_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) being the principal amount due and payable on account of illegal termination; 
(b) Award pre-suit interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum on the said sum from {{TERMINATION_DATE}} till the date of filing of this suit, amounting to Rs. {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_AMOUNT}} (or as may be computed); 
(c) Award pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC at {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum during the pendency of the suit; 
(d) Award future interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from the date of decree till actual realization; 
(e) Award costs of this suit including court fees and legal notice costs; 
(f) Grant such other and further relief as this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.


LIST OF DOCUMENTS

Annexure A – Primary dealership agreement/transaction document; Annexure B – Proof of investment/performance; Annexure C – Correspondence/reminders to Defendant; Annexure D – Legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} with postal receipt and AD card; Annexure E – Defendant's reply to legal notice or proof of non-reply.


VERIFICATION

I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby solemnly verify and state on oath that the contents of paragraphs 1 to 24 of this plaint are true and correct to my personal knowledge based upon the records and documents available with me, that the contents relating to legal submissions are based upon legal advice received and are believed to be correct, that no part of the cause of action has arisen within the jurisdiction of any other court, and that nothing material has been concealed therefrom. Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this the ____ day of __________ 20____.

{{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}

PLAINTIFF

Through:
{{ADVOCATE_NAME}}
Advocate
Enrollment No. {{ADVOCATE_ENROLLMENT}}
{{ADVOCATE_ADDRESS}}
